corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: Additionally, we observed like Chung et al. (2022) that finetuning these models on instructions lead to promising results, and we plan to further investigate this in future work.
Following recent work on large language models, our network is based on the transformer architecture .
Here are the main difference with the original architecture, and where we were found the inspiration for this change (in bracket):
We introduce LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters.

We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using publicly available datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary and inaccessible datasets.
In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 (175B) on most benchmarks, and LLaMA-65B is competitive with the best models, Chinchilla-70B and PaLM-540B.
Large Languages Models (LLMs) trained on massive corpora of texts have shown their ability to perform new tasks from textual instructions or from a few examples .
However, recent work from shows that, for a given compute budget, the best performances are not achieved by the largest models, but by smaller models trained on more data.
The objective of the scaling laws from is to determine how to best scale the dataset and model sizes for a particular training compute budget.
For instance, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks, despite being 10× smaller.
In the rest of this paper, we present an overview of the modifications we made to the transformer architecture , as well as our training method.
In the rest of this paper, we present an overview of the modifications we made to the transformer architecture , as well as our training method.

This task, often framed as next token prediction, has long been considered a core problem in natural language processing . proposed to measure machine intelligence by using language through the "imitation game", language modeling has been proposed as a benchmark to measure progress toward artificial intelligence (Mahoney, 1999).
For the power consumption of a A100-80GB, we take the thermal design power for NVLink systems, that is 400W.

There is a long history of scaling for language models, for both the model and dataset sizes. showed the benefits of using language models trained on 2 trillion tokens, resulting in 300 billion n-grams, on the quality of machine translation.

There is a long history of scaling for language models, for both the model and dataset sizes. showed the benefits of using language models trained on 2 trillion tokens, resulting in 300 billion n-grams, on the quality of machine translation.

This allowed to train a 5-gram model on 975 billions tokens from CommonCrawl, resulting in a model with 500 billions n-grams . introduced the One Billion Word benchmark, a large scale training dataset to measure the progress of language models.

This allowed to train a 5-gram model on 975 billions tokens from CommonCrawl, resulting in a model with 500 billions n-grams . introduced the One Billion Word benchmark, a large scale training dataset to measure the progress of language models.
In the context of neural language models, Jozefowicz et al. (2016) obtained state-of-the-art results on the Billion Word benchmark by scaling LSTMs to 1 billion parameters.
In the context of neural language models, Jozefowicz et al. (2016) obtained state-of-the-art results on the Billion Word benchmark by scaling LSTMs to 1 billion parameters.

This lead to a series of Large Language Models, such as Jurassic-1 , Megatron-Turing NLG , Gopher , Chinchilla , , OPT , and GLM . and studied the impact of scaling on the performance of deep learning models, showing the existence of power laws between the model and dataset sizes and the performance of the system. derived power laws specifically for transformer based language models, which were later refined by , by adapting the learning rate schedule when scaling datasets.
In this paper, we presented a series of language models that are released openly, and competitive with state-of-the-art foundation models.
Most notably, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 while being more than 10× smaller, and LLaMA-65B is competitive with Chinchilla-70B and PaLM-540B.
Unlike previous studies, we show that it is possible to achieve state-of-the-art performance by training exclusively on publicly available data, without resorting to proprietary datasets.
Finally, we plan to release larger models trained on larger pretraining corpora in the future, since we have seen a constant improvement in performance as we were scaling.
